mechanism: Amiodarone may increase QTc-prolonging effects of Haloperidol.
clinical_effect: Amiodarone may increase QTc-prolonging effects of Haloperidol.
management: Consider alternatives to this combination. If use is necessary, monitor for QTc interval prolongation and arrhythmias (including torsades de pointes). Patients with other risk factors (eg, older age, female sex, bradycardia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, heart disease, and higher drug concentrations) or those using IV haloperidol are likely at greater risk for these potentially life-threatening toxicities.
route_dependency: This interaction is likely of greater significance with IV haloperidol, which has a higher risk for prolonging the QT interval.
discussion:
Data characterizing risks of this combination are not available. However, there is considerable evidence that the individual drugs can cause QTc prolongation and/or torsades de pointes (TdP), and a representative selection of that evidence is described here.
A review discusses 70 cases of QTc prolongation and TdP associated with IV haloperidol.1 An analysis of FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) data concluded that haloperidol had a very strong torsadogenic signal, meeting all criteria by which agents were evaluated.2 In another review of FAERS data, amiodarone was one of the 2 drugs most commonly associated with TdP, with 113 TdP cases at least partially attributed to amiodarone.3 Similarly, in an analysis of 2,735 patients with QT prolongation, amiodarone was associated with 16 (18%) of the 89 TdP cases.4 While the majority of studies evaluating the risk of QTc prolongation with haloperidol have evaluated IV haloperidol, 1 study where over 75% of haloperidol use was oral, found that the mean QTc interval increased 23 ms.5
Combined use of drugs that prolong the QTc interval may further increase the risk for serious toxicities, but evidence about the risks with such combinations is limited. The use of multiple QT-prolonging drugs increases the risk score in two different risk models,6,7 and at least one study has identified the use of an additional QT-prolonging drug as the most common risk factor among patients with QT prolongation.1 In another study, of 49 patients with an overridden QT-related drug interaction alert and both a pre- and post-alert ECG, 17 (35%) experienced more than a 30 ms QT interval increase.8 In contrast, several studies report only a limited increase in QT prolongation with concurrent use of QT-prolonging drugs, and that magnitude of risk depends on specific drugs included.9,10,11

mechanism: Aspirin may increase anticoagulant effects of Heparins (Low Molecular Weight).
clinical_effect: Aspirin may increase anticoagulant effects of Heparins (Low Molecular Weight).
management: Although the concomitant use of aspirin and low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) is indicated in a variety of clinical scenarios (eg, acute coronary syndromes, revascularization procedures, ST-elevation MI), concomitant therapy may increase the risk for bleeding. Monitor patients for signs and symptoms of bleeding during coadministration.
discussion:
Coadministration of low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) with lower (less than 325 mg) doses of aspirin is often clinically indicated. Clinical practice guidelines from the American College of Cardiology and the American Heart Association recommend coadministration of an anticoagulant, such as enoxaparin, with antiplatelet therapy, such as aspirin, in all patients with Non-ST-Elevation Acute Coronary Syndromes (ACS).1 Coadministration of enoxaparin and aspirin (typically 325 mg loading dose, then less than 100 mg daily) as part of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) is also recommended in clinical practice guidelines for coronary artery revascularization.2 Patients with ST-elevation myocardial infarction (MI) may also be treated with enoxaparin and aspirin according to clinical practice guidelines.3 Use of LMWH in combination with low dose aspirin has also been found to be more effective than low dose aspirin alone for high risk pregnant patients for the prevention of preeclampsia and preterm birth and fetal growth restriction.4
Although appropriate in some circumstances, coadministration of aspirin and LMWH can increase the risk of bleeding. In a retrospective study of patients receiving combination therapy with aspirin, clopidogrel, and enoxaparin for ACS (n=666) gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding occurred in 2.7% of patients and 1 patient (0.15%) died of a GI bleed.5 Another retrospective study of 13 patients who received enoxaparin and aspirin (81 mg) for thromboprophylaxis found 69% had major bleeding events requiring transfusions.6 Case reports also describe patients who experienced bleeding (eg, hematomas) after coadministration of enoxaparin and low-dose aspirin.7,8,9,10,11

mechanism: FentaNYL may increase serotonergic effects of Linezolid. This could result in serotonin syndrome.
clinical_effect: FentaNYL may increase serotonergic effects of Linezolid. This could result in serotonin syndrome.
management: Consider alternatives to the coadministration of fentanyl and linezolid when possible, as product labeling states the concomitant use of fentanyl with MAOIs such as linezolid, or use of such as agents within 14 days is not recommended. If combined use is necessary, monitor for signs and symptoms of serotonin syndrome/serotonin toxicity (eg, hyperreflexia, clonus, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, tremor, autonomic instability, mental status changes). Patients with other risk factors (eg, higher drug concentrations/doses, greater numbers of serotonergic agents) are likely at greater risk for these potentially life-threatening toxicities.
discussion:
A case report described a 4-year-old patient who started taking fluoxetine (5 mg daily) and 11 days later was started on linezolid (140 mg twice daily).1 After 2 days of concomitant therapy, the patient received fentanyl (200 mcg single oral dose) as premedication for a wound debridement. After the procedure the patient developed myoclonic movements and agitation which worsened after an additional dose of linezolid and was diagnosed with serotonin syndrome/serotonin toxicity (SS/ST). Symptoms resolved after fluoxetine and linezolid discontinuation.1 Other reports also describe patients who experienced SS/ST due to the combined use of linezolid and fentanyl.2,3 Numerous other case reports describe patients who were stable on a single serotonergic agent (eg, SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI) who developed SS/ST after the addition of fentanyl,4,5,6,7 or linezolid.8,9,10,11
Fentanyl prescribing information states that use is not recommended in patients taking MAOIs like linezolid or within 14 days of stopping such treatment.12 Linezolid prescribing information states that linezolid should not be combined with other serotonergic agents if possible.13 Both linezolid and fentanyl prescribing information recommend frequently evaluating the patient, particularly during treatment initiation and dose adjustment if concomitant use is warranted or unavoidable.12,13
Many drugs possess an ability to enhance central serotonergic activity (eg, inhibition of serotonin reuptake, decreased serotonin metabolism, direct serotonin receptor stimulation) either as an intended mechanism of action or as an additional effect. The concomitant use of 2 or more of these agents may increase the risk for serotonin toxicity (also called serotonin syndrome), a condition of serotonergic overstimulation characterized by autonomic, neuromuscular, and neurologic effects.14,15,16

mechanism: Meperidine may increase serotonergic effects of FentaNYL. This could result in serotonin syndrome. Meperidine may increase CNS depressant effects of FentaNYL.
clinical_effect: Meperidine may increase serotonergic effects of FentaNYL. This could result in serotonin syndrome. Meperidine may increase CNS depressant effects of FentaNYL.
management: Consider alternatives to this combination. If use is necessary, monitor for signs and symptoms of serotonin syndrome/serotonin toxicity (eg, hyperreflexia, clonus, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, tremor, autonomic instability, mental status changes) as well as for signs and symptoms of CNS depression (eg, somnolence, respiratory depression).
discussion:
A case report describes a 41-year-old patient who received fentanyl (150 mcg) as part of his anesthesia regimen during a clavicle fracture repair.1 In the post anesthesia care unit he received meperidine (30 mg IV) and immediately developed signs and symptoms of serotonin syndrome/serotonin toxicity (SS/ST) characterized by hypertension, tachycardia and hyperthermia.1 Additionally, there is evidence that meperidine and fentanyl can contribute to the development of SS/ST, and a representative selection of that evidence is described here. Numerous case reports describe patients who were stable on a single serotonergic agent (eg, SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI) who developed SS/ST after the addition of meperidine2,3,4 or fentanyl.5,6,7,8
Many drugs possess an ability to enhance central serotonergic activity (eg, inhibition of serotonin reuptake, decreased serotonin metabolism, direct serotonin receptor stimulation) either as an intended mechanism of action or as an additional effect. The concomitant use of 2 or more of these agents may increase the risk for serotonin toxicity (also called serotonin syndrome), a condition of serotonergic overstimulation characterized by autonomic, neuromuscular, and neurologic effects.9,10,11
In addition to this risk of serotonin syndrome, concomitant use of 2 opioids may significantly increase the risk for CNS depression. A US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) drug safety communication states that combined use of opioids with other drugs that depress the CNS (ie, another opioid analgesic) has resulted in serious side effects, including slowed or difficult breathing and deaths.12 Concomitant use should be avoided when possible and if combined, limit the dosages and duration of each drug to the minimum possible while achieving the desired clinical effect. Warn patients and caregivers about the risk of slowed or difficult breathing and/or sedation.12

mechanism: Amiodarone may increase QTc-prolonging effects of Methadone.
clinical_effect: Amiodarone may increase QTc-prolonging effects of Methadone.
management: Consider alternatives to this combination. If use is necessary, monitor for QTc interval prolongation and arrhythmias (including torsades de pointes). Patients with other risk factors (eg, older age, female sex, bradycardia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, heart disease, and higher drug concentrations) are likely at greater risk for these potentially life-threatening toxicities.

mechanism: Digoxin may increase hyperkalemic effects of Potassium Phosphate.
clinical_effect: Digoxin may increase hyperkalemic effects of Potassium Phosphate.
management: Avoid coadministration of potassium phosphate and digoxin due to the risk of severe and potentially fatal hyperkalemia. This risk is increased in patients with other risk factors, such as renal impairment. If coadministration is required, monitor serum potassium concentrations closely.
discussion:
In a retrospective case-control study of 774 hospitalized patients treated with drugs known to influence serum potassium, use of digoxin was not significantly associated with the development of hyperkalemia, which occurred in 52 (6.7%).1
Prescribing information for potassium phosphates injection state coadministration with digoxin should be avoided due to the risk of severe and potentially fatal hyperkalemia.2,3 This risk is increased in patients with other risk factors, such as renal impairment. If coadministration is required, monitor serum potassium concentrations closely.2,3
The mechanism of this potential interaction is additive hyperkalemic effects. At higher concentrations, digoxin inhibition of NaK-ATPase results in potassium efflux of intracellular potassium, resulting in higher serum potassium levels.4 Hyperkalemia may occur in digoxin overdose with extremely high digoxin serum concentrations, particularly in patients with renal impairment.

mechanism: Propofol may increase CNS depressant effects of FentaNYL.
clinical_effect: Propofol may increase CNS depressant effects of FentaNYL.
management: Consider alternatives to this combination when possible. If this combination is used, monitor more closely for bradycardia, apnea, and other indications of excessive CNS depression. Propofol induction dose requirements may be reduced. Limit doses and duration of each drug as much as possible. Pediatric patients may be at particularly high risk for bradycardia.
discussion:
Specific data regarding this combination are not available, but propofol labeling describes a greater risk for apnea, bradycardia, and other signs of excessive CNS depression with concurrent use of fentanyl.1 Pediatric patients may be at particularly high risk for these side effects.1 The propofol induction dose requirement may also be decreased.
Though not specific to this particular combination, a US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) drug safety communication states that combined use of opioids with benzodiazepines or other drugs that depress the CNS has resulted in serious side effects, including slowed or difficult breathing and deaths.2 A cohort study found that the risk of opioid-related overdose death was 10-fold higher in patients who also received benzodiazepines (7.0 per 10,000 person-years) compared with patients who received opioids alone (0.7 per 10,000 person years).3 In another analysis of opioid-treated patients in the Veterans Health Administration database, the risk of death from drug overdose was significantly higher among those prescribed concomitant benzodiazepines compared with those receiving opioids alone (HR 3.86).4 A cohort study of 4501 patients prescribed buprenorphine or methadone for treatment of opioid dependence reported an increased risk of both overdose-related deaths (adjusted HR 1.49 to 2.02) and all-cause mortality (adjusted HR 1.28 to 2.01) for patients receiving concurrent benzodiazepines, z-drugs (zopiclone, zolpidem, or zaleplon), or pregabalin.5 Not all of these increases were statistically significant, but the overall picture of a general increased risk with these combinations is consistent with other data. Additional analyses have concluded that other CNS depressants (eg, antipsychotics, antidepressants, antiseizure drugs) and alcohol are often implicated in opioid-related overdose and death.6,7

mechanism: Vancomycin may increase nephrotoxic effects of Amphotericin B.
clinical_effect: Vancomycin may increase nephrotoxic effects of Amphotericin B.
management: If coadministration of vancomycin and amphotericin B is required, use caution and monitor renal function closely due to the increased risk of drug-induced nephrotoxicity.
discussion:
A meta-analysis of 53 case-control and cohort studies identified concomitant amphotericin B as a risk factor for vancomycin-associated acute kidney injury (OR 5.21 [95% CI, 3.44 to 7.87]) though formulation of amphotericin B used was not specified.1
Vancomycin labeling recommends exercising caution and intensive monitoring of renal function with coadministration with amphotericin B due to the risk of nephrotoxicity.2 Amphotericin B deoxycholate labeling also recommends intensive monitoring of renal function with coadministration of known nephrotoxic agents.3 Labeling for liposomal and lipid complex formulations of amphotericin B note that no formal clinical studies of drug interactions have been performed.4,5 However, intensive monitoring of renal function is still recommended even though the incidence of renal adverse events is lower using these liposomal and lipid complex formulations.
The mechanism of this interaction is likely additive nephrotoxicity.

mechanism: Calcium Channel Blockers (Nondihydropyridine) may increase bradycardic effects of Amiodarone. Sinus arrest has been reported.
clinical_effect: Calcium Channel Blockers (Nondihydropyridine) may increase bradycardic effects of Amiodarone. Sinus arrest has been reported.
management: Monitor increased effects and toxicities (eg, bradycardia, sinus arrest, decreased cardiac output) if amiodarone is combined with a nondihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (ie, diltiazem, verapamil).
discussion:
A case report describes a 61-year-old female who was administered diltiazem (60 mg four times daily) for 4 days, and then started on amiodarone (600 mg twice daily).1 After 4 days of concomitant therapy, she developed sinus arrest, low cardiac output, and oliguria. She required immediate discontinuation of both medications, ventricular pacing, and intravenous dopamine to stabilize her hemodynamics. Four days later she was started on amiodarone alone which she tolerated without incident.1
Amiodarone prescribing information states that nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers could potentiate the electrophysiologic and hemodynamic effects of amiodarone, resulting in bradycardia, sinus arrest, and atrioventricular (AV) block.2 Close monitoring for these adverse events is recommended if these agents are combined.2
The mechanism of this interaction is likely due to the additive effects of amiodarone and nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers on both the sinoatrial and AV nodes.
footnotes:
1. Lee TH, Friedman PL, Goldman L, et al. Sinus arrest and hypotension with combined amiodarone-diltiazem therapy. Am Heart J 1985; 109:163.[PubMed 3966317]

mechanism: Calcium Channel Blockers (Nondihydropyridine) may increase antiplatelet effects of Aspirin.
clinical_effect: Calcium Channel Blockers (Nondihydropyridine) may increase antiplatelet effects of Aspirin.
management: Monitor for increased antiplatelet effects of aspirin (eg, increased bruising or bleeding) if combined with nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers.
discussion:
The bleeding time of a patient taking aspirin (650 mg several times per week for several years) was twice as long after initiation of verapamil (80 mg three times daily), with abnormal bruising that resolved after verapamil discontinuation.1 Verapamil was re-initiated without aspirin and no bleeding or bruising recurred.1 Another case report describes a patient taking aspirin (325 mg daily) who developed ecchymosis of the extremities and a retroperitoneal bleed approximately 3 weeks after starting verapamil (240 mg daily).2 Bleeding times also doubled in 5 healthy volunteers given oral diltiazem (60 mg every 8 hours x 7 doses) after aspirin administration (40 mg daily x 3 doses).3 Other investigations found that the addition of diltiazem or clentiazem to aspirin and ticlopidine therapy resulted in inhibition of thromboxane B2 production and reduced platelet aggregation when compared with aspirin and ticlopidine therapy alone,4,5 and inhibition of platelet aggregation by aspirin was enhanced by the addition of diltiazem in another studies.6 In contrast, 2 studies found that diltiazem or verapamil alone inhibited platelet aggregation,7,8 but did not enhance the effects of aspirin, reduce thromboxane B2 levels, or prolong bleeding times.8
Verapamil prescribing information states that cases of increased bleeding have been reported with verapamil and aspirin coadministration.9
The mechanism of these interactions are unknown, although it has been hypothesized that calcium channel blockers inhibit thromboxane B2 production and/or interfere with platelet calcium metabolism.

mechanism: Magnesium Sulfate may increase adverse/toxic effects of Calcium Channel Blockers (Dihydropyridine). Specifically, the risk of hypotension or muscle weakness may be increased.
clinical_effect: Magnesium Sulfate may increase adverse/toxic effects of Calcium Channel Blockers (Dihydropyridine). Specifically, the risk of hypotension or muscle weakness may be increased.
management: Monitor carefully for hypotension or muscle weakness in patients receiving dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers and magnesium sulfate. Pregnant patients and those receiving parenteral magnesium sulfate are at higher risk. Rare cases of muscular paralysis have been reported in pregnant patients receiving oral nifedipine and parenteral magnesium for the treatment of pre-eclampsia.
discussion:
A retrospective case-control study found exposure to magnesium sulfate and nifedipine significantly increased the risk of pulmonary edema among pregnant women in preterm labor.1 Two case reports describe the development of muscular paralysis in pregnant women being treated for pre-eclampsia with concomitant oral nifedipine and parenteral magnesium sulfate.2,3 In one case, the symptoms dissipated within 25 minutes of stopping the magnesium, and in the other administration of IV calcium gluconate reversed the paralytic state. Two other pregnant patients (receiving methyldopa and IV magnesium) experienced transient hypotension within 1 hour following the administration of oral nifedipine.4
In contrast to these case reports, multiple studies have concluded that the combination of magnesium sulfate and dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers can be safe and effective. Prospective studies in women with eclampsia5 and gestational hypertension6 reported that the combination of magnesium sulfate and nifedipine exerted beneficial synergistic effects, resulting in reduced maternal and perinatal morbidity and mortality5 and reduced premature birth, cesarean section, and neonatal asphyxia.6 A randomized controlled trial of 120 patients with subarachnoid hemorrhage found patients who received concomitant magnesium sulfate and nimodipine (20 mg 4 times daily for 21 days) had significantly fewer cerebral vasospasms, delayed cerebral ischemia, secondary cerebral infarction, and neurological deficits compared to patients who received magnesium sulfate alone.7 A retrospective chart review of women being treated for preeclampsia evaluated neuromuscular and cardiovascular adverse events in women who had received intravenous magnesium sulfate either with nifedipine (cases; n=162) or with another anti-hypertensive or no anti-hypertensive (controls; n=215).8 There was no significant difference in reports of neuromuscular weakness between cases and controls (53.1% vs. 46.0%, respectively).

mechanism: Calcium Salts may decrease therapeutic effects of DOBUTamine.
clinical_effect: Calcium Salts may decrease therapeutic effects of DOBUTamine.
management: Monitor for decreased therapeutic effects of dobutamine (specifically cardiac output) if a calcium supplement (especially IV) is initiated/dose increased.
route_dependency: This interaction is likely most clinically significant with IV calcium administration.
discussion: A study in 22 patients (status post coronary bypass surgery) receiving dobutamine (2.5-5 mcg/kg/min) experienced a 30% reduction in cardiac output with concomitant administration of calcium chloride by continuous infusion (1 mg/kg/min, subsequently reduced to 0.25 mg/kg/min).1 The patients in the study had a normal cardiac output prior to calcium administration. Milrinone was not affected by the calcium administration, and thus might be a lower risk alternative to dobutamine. The mechanism of this interaction is unknown. The authors of the report suggest that calcium may interfere with signal transduction through the beta-adrenergic receptor complex.
footnotes: 1. Butterworth JF 4th, Zaloga GP, Prielipp RC, et al. Calcium inhibits the cardiac stimulating properties of dobutamine but not of amrinone. Chest 1992; 101:174.[PubMed 1729065]

mechanism: Atropine (Systemic) may increase therapeutic effects of EPHEDrine (Systemic).
clinical_effect: Atropine (Systemic) may increase therapeutic effects of EPHEDrine (Systemic).
management: Monitor closely for evidence of exessive blood pressure increase with this combination.
discussion:
The labeling for injectable ephedrine states that concurrent use of atropine may result in an enhanced pressor response to ephedrine administration.1
The specific mechanism for this purported interaction is not specified, but atropine would be expected to have some additive or synergistic actions resulting from its parasympathetolytic actions, likely enhancing the effects of ephedrine.
footnotes: 1. Akovaz (Ephedrine) [product labeling]. Chesterfield, MO, USA: Eclat Pharmaceuticals, April 2016.

mechanism: EPHEDrine (Systemic) may decrease serum concentrations of DexAMETHasone (Systemic).
clinical_effect: EPHEDrine (Systemic) may decrease serum concentrations of DexAMETHasone (Systemic).
management: Monitor for loss of dexamethasone therapeutic effects and consider dexamethasone dose increases when combined with ephedrine.
discussion:
In a controlled study of 9 patients with asthma, ephedrine (100 mg daily) decreased the mean dexamethasone (1 mg to 2.6 mg single dose) half-life by 36% and increased the mean metabolic clearance rate by 42%.1 Pulmonary function was not significantly changed during coadministration of dexamethasone and ephedrine.
Prescribing information for dexamethasone states that ephedrine may decrease dexamethasone exposure, which may result in a loss of efficacy. Monitor for loss of dexamethasone therapeutic effects and consider dexamethasone dose increases when combined with ephedrine.2,3
The mechanism of this potential interaction is unknown.
footnotes:
1. Brooks SM, Sholiton LJ, Werk EE Jr, Altenau P. The effects of ephedrine and theophylline on dexamethasone metabolism in bronchial asthma. J Clin Pharmacol 1977; 17:308.[PubMed 870547]
2. Dexamethasone [product labeling]. Eatontown, NJ, USA: West-Ward Pharmaceuticals Corp., August 2016.
3. Hemady (Dexamethasone) [product labeling]. ISRAEL: Dexcel Pharma Technologies Ltd, October 2019.

mechanism: Furosemide may decrease therapeutic effects of Norepinephrine.
clinical_effect: Furosemide may decrease therapeutic effects of Norepinephrine.
management: Monitor blood pressure or mean arterial pressure during coadministration of furosemide and norepinephrine.
discussion:
Prescribing information for some furosemide products state furosemide may reduce the vasoconstricting effect of norepinephrine, and recommends monitoring blood pressure or mean arterial pressure in patients receiving this combination.1,2
The mechanism of this interaction is likely opposing effects of furosemide or norepinephrine on vasoconstriction.
footnotes:

2. Lasix ONYU (furosemide) [product labeling]. Burlington, MA, USA: SQ Innovation, Inc., October 2025.

mechanism: Haloperidol may decrease vasoconstricting effects of EPINEPHrine (Systemic).
clinical_effect: Haloperidol may decrease vasoconstricting effects of EPINEPHrine (Systemic).
management: Consider alternatives to this combination and monitor for reduced epinephrine efficacy, and possible paradoxical effects (ie, hypotension), when combined with haloperidol. Use of alternative vasopressor agents (eg, phenylephrine, metaraminol, norepinephrine) is preferred, especially when using epinephrine for the treatment of hypotension.
discussion:
No studies have documented the effects of haloperidol on the efficacy of epinephrine. However, in patients on chronic oral chlorpromazine (another typical antipsychotic), an IV infusion of epinephrine was associated with a lowering of diastolic blood pressure and an increase in systolic pressure and tachycardia.1 In other studies, IV administration of chlorpromazine to patients receiving epinephrine often resulted in a fall in blood pressure;2 however, this effect rarely occurred when patients received chlorpromazine orally.3
The haloperidol prescribing information states that in patients taking haloperidol who require a vasopressor for the treatment of hypotension, epinephrine should not be used.4 Haloperidol may block its vasopressor activity, and paradoxically further lower blood pressure. Metaraminol, phenylephrine, or norepinephrine should be used instead.4
Haloperidol may block the peripheral alpha effects of the epinephrine, leaving the beta (vasodilating) effects unopposed.

mechanism: Heparins (Low Molecular Weight) may increase hyperkalemic effects of Potassium Salts.
clinical_effect: Heparins (Low Molecular Weight) may increase hyperkalemic effects of Potassium Salts.
management: Monitor serum potassium concentrations closely in patients receiving potassium salts in combination with low molecular weight heparins.
discussion:
In a prospective study of 85 patients, mean serum potassium levels increased 0.17 mmol/L (from 4.26 to 4.43 mmol/L) after 3 days of enoxaparin treatment.1 Serum potassium levels greater than 5 mmol/L were reported in 9% of patients but no patients developed symptomatic hyperkalemia. Increased potassium levels have also been reported in several other studies and reports of patients treated with low molecular weight heparins.2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9
Enoxaparin prescribing information states cases of hyperkalemia have been reported in patients treated with enoxaparin, and that most have occurred in patients with conditions or receiving concomitant medications that increase the risk of hyperkalemia.10 Bemiparin labeling recommends close monitoring of serum potassium during coadministration with drugs that increase serum potassium.11
The mechanism of this interaction is likely additive risk of hyperkalemia.

mechanism: Amiodarone may increase serum concentrations of Lidocaine (Systemic).
clinical_effect: Amiodarone may increase serum concentrations of Lidocaine (Systemic).
management: Monitor for increased serum concentrations/toxicity (eg, bradycardia, seizure) of lidocaine if amiodarone is initiated/dose increased. Monitor for decreased serum concentrations/effects of lidocaine if amiodarone is discontinued/dose decreased. Amiodarone doses less than 400 mg/day may be of limited concern. Larger doses (eg, 1200 mg/day) may cause significant changes in lidocaine pharmacokinetics.
discussion:
A study of 6 patients with cardiac arrhythmias found coadministration of lidocaine (1 mg/kg) increased the trough concentration of amiodarone 1.24-fold after a loading dose of amiodarone (3 g over 6 days).1 Additionally, a case report describes a patient who experienced a seizure and whose serum lidocaine concentration increased to 12.6 mcg/mL from 5.4 mcg/mL (therapeutic range, 1.5 to 5 mcg/mL)approximately 65 hours after initiating amiodarone therapy (600 mg twice daily).2 Another case report describes a patient who initiated with amiodarone (600 mg/day) for rapid atrial flutter who received local anesthesia with lidocaine (2%, 15 mL) for insertion of a permanent pacemaker and within 25 minutes developed severe sinus bradycardia, sinoatrial arrest, and unconsciousness.3 The patient recovered with cardiac massage, atropine, and isoproterenol.
In contrast, the pharmacokinetics of lidocaine (1 mg/kg IV bolus administered over 2 minutes) were unaltered in 10 patients when administered following a 4- to 5-week course of amiodarone (200-400 mg/day).4
Amiodarone prescribing information states lidocaine metabolism may be inhibited by amiodarone, which can lead to bradycardia when lidocaine is given for local anesthesia or seizure when lidocaine is used intravenously.5 Monitoring for supratherapeutic lidocaine concentrations and adverse effects (eg, bradycardia, seizure) is likely warranted when these agents are combined.
The mechanism of this interaction is likely amiodarone inhibition of CYP3A4, an enzyme involved in lidocaine metabolism. The effect may be dose related, noting that 600 mg twice daily had a significant effect, whereas 400 mg/day demonstrated no effect.

mechanism: Metoclopramide may increase CNS depressant effects of DiazePAM. Metoclopramide may increase serum concentrations of DiazePAM.
clinical_effect: Metoclopramide may increase CNS depressant effects of DiazePAM. Metoclopramide may increase serum concentrations of DiazePAM.
management: Monitor patients for increased CNS depressant effects (eg, somnolence, drowsiness) if metoclopramide is combined with diazepam. Consider avoiding metoclopramide or diazepam, depending on the importance of the drug to the patient.
discussion:
Metoclopramide prescribing information states that metoclopramide may impair the mental and/or physical abilities required for the performance of hazardous tasks such as operating machinery or driving a motor vehicle.1 Concomitant use of central nervous system (CNS) depressants, such as diazepam, may increase these risks.1 Consider avoiding metoclopramide or the CNS depressant, depending on the importance of the drug to the patient.1
In addition to the potential for additive CNS depressant effects, IV metoclopramide has been reported to increase peak serum concentrations of diazepam by approximately 38%, and the time to peak was reduced from 60 to 20 minutes.2 Similar results were not found in a separate study using oral metoclopramide.3 The clinical significance of this increase in diazepam exposure is likely minimal.
The mechanism of increased diazepam exposure during coadministration with metoclopramide is unknown.
footnotes:
1. Reglan (Metoclopramide) [product labeling]. Baudette, MN, USA: ANI Pharmaceuticals Inc, August 2017.
2. Gamble JA, Gaston JH, Nair SG, Dundee JW. Some pharmacological factors influencing the absorption of diazepam following oral administration. Br J Anaesth 1976; 48:1181.[PubMed 1023954]
3. Chapman MH, Woolner DF, Begg EJ, et al. Co-administered oral metoclopramide does not enhance the rate of absorption of oral diazepam. Anaesth Intensive Care 1988; 16:202.[PubMed 3394914]

mechanism: Nitroglycerin may decrease anticoagulant effects of Heparin. Nitroglycerin may decrease serum concentrations of Heparin.
clinical_effect: Nitroglycerin may decrease anticoagulant effects of Heparin. Nitroglycerin may decrease serum concentrations of Heparin.
management: Monitor response to heparin closely when used in combination with nitroglycerin. An association between nitroglycerin use (particularly higher doses and the intravenous formulation) and diminished heparin response has been reported; however, many studies have also found no significant interaction between heparin and nitroglycerin.
discussion:
One report describes both clinical heparin resistance (measured using activated partial thromboplastin time, aPTT) in 7 patients receiving infusions of both heparin and intravenous nitroglycerin (NTG), and an increased heparin sensitivity following the cessation of NTG.1 A study evaluating heparin response in patients also receiving low-dose NTG (n=42) noted that both median aPTT and heparin concentrations were lower at 4 hours after the heparin infusion was started than at the 2-hour time point, despite a constant heparin infusion rate.2 Heparin prescribing information states intravenous nitroglycerin may decrease the partial thromboplastin time in patients receiving heparin, with a rebound effect after nitroglycerin discontinuation.3 When using heparin and intravenous concomitantly, heparin prescribing information recommends monitoring of partial thromboplastin time; dose adjustments may be required.3
In a group of 20 patients not receiving NTG there were no differences in heparin response or concentrations between the 2-hour and 4-hour measurements.2 A study comparing heparin response between patients (n=50) randomized to concurrent NTG or isosorbide dinitrate (ISDN) similarly reported a lower aPTT ratio and lower heparin concentrations at 4 hours in the group receiving NTG (both vs. the 4-hour values in the ISDN group and vs. the 2-hour values in the NTG group).4 The groups were not significantly different at other time points, suggesting a particular need for monitoring early in combination therapy. Other studies have noted evidence of an interaction but only at higher NTG doses and/or infusion rates.5,6
In contrast with the reports described above, many in vitro7,8,9,10 and clinical studies9,10,11,12,13,14,15 have evaluated this possible interaction and found no evidence of a clinically significant interaction or evidence of a possible small, but likely clinically insignificant interaction.6,16
The exact mechanism of this possible interaction is uncertain, but

2. Brack MJ, More RS, Hubner PJ, Gershlick AH. The effect of low dose nitroglycerin on plasma heparin concentrations and activated partial thromboplastin times. Blood Coagul Fibrinolysis 1993; 4:183.[PubMed 8457649]

mechanism: Nitroglycerin may increase hypotensive effects of Apomorphine.
clinical_effect: Nitroglycerin may increase hypotensive effects of Apomorphine.
management: Patients taking apomorphine should lie down before and after taking sublingual nitroglycerin. Monitor blood pressure for hypotension and orthostatic hypotension when these agents are combined.
discussion:
The apomorphine prescribing information states that in a study of healthy subjects, the hypotensive effect of apomorphine on systolic and diastolic blood pressure was exacerbated by the concomitant use of sublingual nitroglycerin.1 When nitroglycerin (0.4 mg) and apomorphine were combined, the mean largest decrease (the mean of each subject’s largest drop in blood pressure) in supine systolic and diastolic blood pressure was 9.7 mm Hg and 9.3 mm Hg, respectively.1. The mean largest decrease in standing systolic and diastolic blood pressure was 14.3 mm Hg and 13.5 mm Hg, respectively.1 Some individuals experienced very large decreases in standing systolic and diastolic blood pressure, up to a maximum decrease of 65 mm Hg and 43 mm Hg, respectively. In comparison, the mean largest decrease in supine systolic and diastolic blood pressure when apomorphine was administered alone was 6.1 mm Hg and 7.3 mm Hg, respectively, and in standing systolic and diastolic blood pressure was 6.7 mm Hg and 8.4 mm Hg, respectively.1
Patients taking apomorphine should lie down before and after taking sublingual nitroglycerin.1 Monitor blood pressure for hypotension and orthostatic hypotension in patients taking apomorphine with concomitant antihypertensive medications or vasodilators.1

mechanism: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of Acetaminophen. Specifically, serum concentrations of acetaminophen may be decreased (leading to decreased efficacy), but the formation of the toxic N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI) metabolite may be increased (leading to increased hepatotoxicity).
clinical_effect: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of Acetaminophen. Specifically, serum concentrations of acetaminophen may be decreased (leading to decreased efficacy), but the formation of the toxic N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI) metabolite may be increased (leading to increased hepatotoxicity).
management: Monitor for acetaminophen induced hepatotoxicity if a large dose (or long term use) of acetaminophen is used in a patient taking phenytoin or fosphenytoin. Decreased effects of acetaminophen may also be seen.
discussion:
In one study, the steady-state plasma concentration of acetaminophen (APAP) was 17% lower after treatment with phenytoin (100 mg every 8 hours for 11 days).1 In other clinical studies comparing patients with epilepsy taking phenytoin (either alone or combined with other agents) with healthy controls, the APAP AUC was 31% to 41% lower, clearance was 46% higher, and the half-life was 21% to 28% shorter in the epileptic patients.2,3,4 This decreased exposure could reduce therapeutic response to acetaminophen, but such an effect has not been reported. Of more concern is the possibility of increased production of the toxic acetaminophen metabolite (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine [NAPQI]) in the presence of phenytoin therapy. Several case reports describe patients taking phenytoin who experienced liver toxicity (ranging from elevations in liver function tests, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, fulminant liver failure requiring transplantation, and death) after acute ingestion of acetaminophen (25 g to 45 g).5,6,7 In all cases, acetaminophen levels were relatively low and only one patient received N-acetylcysteine (NAC). The authors of each report suggest that the severity of liver dysfunction experienced was out of proportion to the measured serum APAP level suggesting phenytoin played a contributory role. Another case report describes a patient on phenytoin therapy who experienced elevations in liver function tests after ingesting 1,300 mg to 6,200 mg of APAP daily for 10 days.8 Phenytoin concentrations do not appear to be affected by acetaminophen.9
The exact mechanism of an interaction between acetaminophen and phenytoin is not clear. Normally, 80% to 90% of APAP undergoes conjugation via 5-diphosphate-glucuronosyltranferases (UGTs) to inactive, nontoxic metabolites. Approximately 10% of the dose is oxidized via CYP enzymes to the toxic NAPQI metabolite. NAPQI is rapidly conjugated, in the presence of adequate stores of glutathione, to form additional nontoxic metabolites t

mechanism: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of Amiodarone. Amiodarone may increase serum concentrations of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin.
clinical_effect: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of Amiodarone. Amiodarone may increase serum concentrations of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin.
management: Monitor for increased serum concentrations and toxicity of phenytoin if amiodarone is initiated or dose increased, and decreased serum concentrations and effects if amiodarone is discontinued or dose decreased. Monitor for decreased amiodarone serum concentrations and effects if phenytoin is initiated or dose increased, and increased serum concentrations and effects if phenytoin is discontinued or dose decreased. Maximal reductions in amiodarone exposure are expected to occur after multiple doses of phenytoin have been administered, within days to weeks of initiation. Induction effects may also persist for days to weeks after phenytoin has been discontinued.
discussion:
Pharmacokinetic studies in healthy volunteers found amiodarone (200 mg/day) for 3 to 6 weeks increased phenytoin serum concentrations and AUC 1.4-fold.1,2 Serum phenytoin concentrations have been reported to increase two to fourfold within a few weeks of initiating amiodarone therapy (400-1200 mg/day) with resultant phenytoin toxicity.3,4,5 A physiologically based pharmacokinetic modeling study predicted the AUC of phenytoin would increase 23% to 29% with concomitant amiodarone and reducing the phenytoin dose 45% would achieve a phenytoin AUC similar to that observed with phenytoin alone.6 Additionally, a case report describes a 66 year-old patient treated with phenytoin (300 mg daily) for 10 years who developed symptoms of phenytoin toxicity (difficulty walking, blurred vision, leg weakness) with a supratherapeutic phenytoin concentration of 41 mcg/mL 1 month after initiating amiodarone (800 mg daily for 2 weeks then 400 mg daily).7
In addition to the effects on phenytoin, serum amiodarone concentrations have been noted to decrease during concomitant therapy. In a 6 week study, phenytoin administration (2 to 4 mg/kg/day) lead serum amiodarone concentrations to decrease by 33% to 49% below predicted values in weeks 5 and 6, respectively.8
Phenytoin prescribing information states that amiodarone may increase phenytoin concentrations.9 Amiodarone prescribing information similarly says that amiodarone may increase phenytoin concentrations and recommends monitoring phenytoin levels.10 Amiodarone prescribing information also states that cytochrome P450 inducers may decrease serum amiodarone levels.
Amiodarone likely increases phenytoin exposure by inhibiting CYP2C9, an enzyme responsible for phenytoin metabolism. Phenytoin likely decreases amiodarone exposure via induction of CYP1A2 and CYP3A4, enzymes responsible for amiodarone metabolism.

mechanism: Calcium Channel Blockers (Nondihydropyridine) may increase serum concentrations of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin. Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of Calcium Channel Blockers (Nondihydropyridine).
clinical_effect: Calcium Channel Blockers (Nondihydropyridine) may increase serum concentrations of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin. Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of Calcium Channel Blockers (Nondihydropyridine).
management: Consider alternatives to this combination when possible. If combined, monitor for increased phenytoin concentrations and toxicities and monitor for decreased calcium channel blocker efficacy.
discussion:
Signs of phenytoin toxicity developed within 2 weeks of initiating diltiazem therapy in a patient who had been receiving phenytoin according to a published case report.1 Similarly, adverse effects were also reported in 3 of 16 patients receiving phenytoin with diltiazem, verapamil, or nifedipine (all adverse effects were reported in diltiazem-treated patients) in a retrospective analysis of patients receiving antiseizure agents together with calcium channel blockers.2
Additionally, phenytoin is a strong CYP3A4 inducer and may decrease serum concentrations and effects of verapamil and diltiazem, both of which are CYP3A4 substrates. In 2 separate pharmacokinetic studies, the strong CYP3A4 inducer rifampin (600 mg daily) coadministered with oral verapamil (120 mg twice daily) decreased the total verapamil AUC 93%, increased the R-verapamil clearance 32-fold, and increased the S-verapamil clearance 57-fold.3,4 Use of rifampin also prevented P-R interval changes seen with verapamil administration at baseline.4 Lastly, a case report demonstrated that concomitant use of phenytoin and verapamil can lower verapamil to subtherapeutic or undetectable levels and eliminate its therapeutic effect.5
The diltiazem and verapamil prescribing information state that CYP3A4 inducers, such as fosphenytoin or phenytoin, may decrease calcium channel blocker concentrations and effects.6,7 The diltiazem prescribing information also states that concomitant use with strong CYP3A4 inducers should be avoided when possible.7
The mechanism by which verapamil and diltiazem increase phenytoin concentrations may be due to inhibition of CYP3A4, an enzyme partially responsible for phenytoin metabolism. The mechanism by which phenytoin decreases verapamil and diltiazem concentrations is due to induction of CYP3A4, an enzyme responsible for verapamil and diltiazem metabolism.

mechanism: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of DiazePAM. DiazePAM may increase serum concentrations of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin.
clinical_effect: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of DiazePAM. DiazePAM may increase serum concentrations of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin.
management: Monitor for reduced diazepam efficacy if combined with fosphenytoin or phenytoin. Additionally, consider monitoring for increased phenytoin serum concentrations and effects when combined with diazepam.
discussion:
The effects of the strong CYP3A4 inducer phenytoin on diazepam pharmacokinetics have not been evaluated in a clinical study. However, in a pharmacokinetic study of 21 healthy volunteers, the strong CYP3A4 inducer rifampin (600 mg daily or 1,200 mg daily) decreased the diazepam (10 mg single oral dose) AUC 73% to 77%.1 In another study, 7 patients with tuberculosis treated with a 3 drug regimen (rifampin, isoniazid, ethambutol) given a single dose of diazepam (7.5 mg IV) had a diazepam clearance that was 4-fold higher than healthy controls not treated for tuberculosis given the same dose of diazepam.2 Diazepam clearance was unchanged when administered with isoniazid alone or ethambutol alone, suggesting rifampin was the drug responsible for the drug interaction.2
In addition to a potential decrease in diazepam exposure and effects, diazepam has been reported to increase concentrations and effects of phenytoin. In a study of 25 phenytoin-stable patients (doses ranged from 300 to 400 mg/day) serum phenytoin concentrations were approximately 80% higher in patients also received either chlordiazepoxide or diazepam.3 One other report describes increased serum phenytoin concentrations and toxicities in a patient who received concomitant diazepam,4 while another study found no change in phenytoin concentrations when coadministered with diazepam.5
Induction of CYP3A4, an enzyme responsible for diazepam metabolism, is likely responsible for the expected decrease in diazepam concentrations when combined with phenytoin. The mechanism by which diazepam could increase phenytoin concentrations is unknown, but competition for CYP2C19, an enzyme responsible for the metabolism of both drugs, may contribute.

mechanism: DOPamine may increase hypotensive effects of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin.
clinical_effect: DOPamine may increase hypotensive effects of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin.
management: Consider monitoring for decreased blood pressure in patients receiving intravenous phenytoin. Hemodynamically unstable patients, including those requiring dopamine to maintain adequate blood pressure, are at elevated risk.
discussion:
A case series describes 5 reports of significant and rapid reductions in blood pressure (previously maintained via a dopamine infusion) following the initiation of phenytoin infusion for seizure control.1 One patient died from cardiopulmonary arrest. Another patient (receiving dopamine and dobutamine) did not experience any blood pressure change when a phenytoin infusion was initiated.2
The mechanism of this possible interaction is unknown. Hypotension is a known possible adverse effect of intravenous phenytoin administration, particularly in hemodynamically unstable patients, so the contribution of dopamine in producing this effect is questionable.

mechanism: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease diuretic effects of Furosemide.
clinical_effect: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease diuretic effects of Furosemide.
management: Monitor for decreased therapeutic effects of furosemide if phenytoin or fosphenytoin is initiated/dose increased, or increased effects if phenytoin or fosphenytoin is discontinued/dose decreased. Maximal reductions in furosemide exposure are expected to occur after multiple doses of fosphenytoin or phenytoin have been administered, within days to weeks of initiation. Induction effects may also persist for days to weeks after the fosphenytoin or phenytoin has been discontinued.
discussion:
The absorption of furosemide (20 mg orally) was reduced by approximately 50% when administered following a 10-day course of phenytoin (300 mg/day) in a study of 5 healthy volunteers.1 The associated diuretic response to furosemide was also reduced with phenytoin. Another study (n=17) reported similar findings of diminished (and delayed) diuretic response to furosemide with concurrent antiseizure therapy (all subjects were receiving phenytoin and phenobarbital, some were also receiving other antiseizure medications).2 In a case report of a critically ill patient with multi-organ failure, resistance to furosemide (given both orally and intravenously) was observed during concurrent therapy with oral phenytoin, with improved response to furosemide observed with discontinuation of phenytoin (which was changed to phenobarbital 100 mg/day).3 However, many other factors could have contributed to furosemide resistance in this case, so it isn't possible to completely attribute the furosemide resistance to an interaction with phenytoin. Similarly, a study in rats showed that the diuretic effect of furosemide was significantly reduced by phenytoin 2 weeks after coadministration.4
The labeling of both phenytoin and furosemide caution that phenytoin impairs the diuretic efficacy of furosemide.5,6
The mechanism of this interaction is uncertain but is thought to involve phenytoin reducing the GI absorption of furosemide as well as interfering with the renal action of furosemide.4,6

mechanism: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of LevETIRAcetam.
clinical_effect: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease serum concentrations of LevETIRAcetam.
management: Monitor closely for evidence of reduced levetiracetam concentrations or effectiveness.
discussion:
Several studies have concluded that concurrent use of enzyme-inducing antiseizure drugs, such as phenytoin, results in a small-to-moderate (ie, 7% to 47%) increases in levetiracetam clearance, and/or reductions in levetiracetam concentrations or concentration to dose ratios that are of uncertain clinical significance.1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8 Most of these studies examined enzyme-inducing antiseizure drugs in aggregate, including most often carbamazepine and phenobarbital.
Prescribing information for at least one levetiracetam formulation states immediate-release levetiracetam tablets (3,000 mg daily) had no effect on phenytoin pharmacokinetics in patients with refractory epilepsy, and levetiracetam pharmacokinetics were not affected by phenytoin.9
The precise impact of phenytoin versus other enzyme-inducing antiseizure drugs is unclear. The specific mechanism by which phenytoin or other enzyme-inducing antiseizure drugs increase levetiracetam clearance is unknown.

mechanism: ALfentanil may increase adverse/toxic effects of Propofol. Specifically the development of opisthotonus (severe hyperextension and spasticity resulting in arching or bridging position) and/or tonic clonic seizures.
clinical_effect: ALfentanil may increase adverse/toxic effects of Propofol. Specifically the development of opisthotonus (severe hyperextension and spasticity resulting in arching or bridging position) and/or tonic clonic seizures.
management: Monitor for increased toxic effects of propofol if coadministered with alfentanil.
discussion: Coadministration of propofol and alfentanil has resulted in opisthotonus (severe hyperextension and spasticity resulting in arching or bridging position) and tonic clonic seizures in patients without history of epilepsy.1,2 In the presence of alfentanil, reduced doses of propofol have been needed to achieve loss of consciousness and suppress the eyelash reflex. A greater hypotensive and heart rate response to propofol has also been noted.3

mechanism: Propofol may increase therapeutic effects of EPHEDrine (Systemic).
clinical_effect: Propofol may increase therapeutic effects of EPHEDrine (Systemic).
management: Monitor closely for evidence of exessive blood pressure increase with this combination.
discussion:
Several studies have reported an increased pressor response with ephedrine in patients receiving concurrent propofol,1,2 and clonidine may further increase this risk when used together with propofol.2,3
The clinical significance of this effect is uncertain, but the labeling for injectable ephedrine warns about the possible enhanced pressor effect with this combination.4
The specific mechanism for this purported interaction is uncertain.

mechanism: EPHEDrine (Systemic) may increase therapeutic effects of Rocuronium.
clinical_effect: EPHEDrine (Systemic) may increase therapeutic effects of Rocuronium.
management: Monitor closely for a more rapid clinical response to rocuronium in patients who receive ephedrine.
discussion:
Pre-treatment with ephedrine was associated with a significantly faster onset of rocuronium effects, shortening the time to onset by an average of 22.3 seconds (22% reduction), according to a meta-analysis of 5 studies involving 282 patients.1
The specific mechanism of this reported interaction has been reported to be an increase in cardiac output caused by ephedrine.2

mechanism: Rocuronium may decrease therapeutic effects of LevETIRAcetam. Specifically, the onset of rocuronium activity may be delayed.
clinical_effect: Rocuronium may decrease therapeutic effects of LevETIRAcetam. Specifically, the onset of rocuronium activity may be delayed.
management: No action required beyond standard clinical care measures.
discussion:
A case report describes a 33 year-old patient with intractable epilepsy (eg, weekly seizures) treated with levetiracetam (1,700 mg twice daily) for over 10 years who experienced delayed rocuronium onset.1 While undergoing generation anesthesia for a planned dental surgery, rocuronium (0.66 mg/kg) was administered and it took over 9 minutes after administration to achieve a train-of-four score of 0 (typical time of onset is 2 minutes).
The mechanism by which levetiracetam may delay rocuronium onset, or whether this case represents an interaction between rocuronium and levetiracetam is unknown. Other antiseizure agents (eg, phenytoin) have been reported to increase alpha-1 glycoprotein (AAG), which promotes binding of cationic drugs, such nondepolarizing muscle relaxants, including rocuronium.1 While it is unknown if levetiracetam similarly increases AAG, AAG levels may be higher in patients with frequent seizures and the authors of this report speculate that recent frequent seizure activity in this patient may have caused increased AAG levels, leading to increased rocuronium protein binding, decreased free unbound rocuronium, and delayed muscle relaxant effects.
footnotes: 1. Yamamoto T, Koyama Y, Tanaka Y, Seo K. Delayed Rocuronium Onset in a Patient Taking Levetiracetam for Epilepsy: A Case Report. Anesth Prog 2024; 71:76.[PubMed 39503128]

mechanism: Amiodarone may decrease therapeutic effects of Sodium Perchlorate.
clinical_effect: Amiodarone may decrease therapeutic effects of Sodium Perchlorate.
management: Use caution if sodium perchlorate is coadministered with or following treatment with amiodarone, as sodium perchlorate efficacy may be reduced.
discussion:
Data evaluating coadministration of sodium perchlorate and amiodarone are not available. However, sodium perchlorate product labeling states coadministration with iodine-containing drugs, such as amiodarone, reduces sodium perchlorate efficacy.1
According to a review article summarizing the effects of amiodarone on thyroid function, amiodarone contains significant amounts of iodine and has intrinsic effects on the thyroid that can lead to hypo- or hyperthyroidism.2 Each molecule of amiodarone contains 2 iodine atoms, meaning that patients taking 200mg of amiodarone daily consume 75 mg of organic iodine, which equates to a daily ingestion that is several-fold greater than the average level of iodine intake.2 Considering the extremely long half-life of amiodarone (several weeks to months), this excess iodine could persist for a long time.
footnotes:
1. Irenat (Sodium Perchlorate) [product labeling]. Dublin, IRELAND: Alliance Pharma (Ireland) Ltd., January 2019.
2. Basaria S, Cooper DS. Amiodarone and the thyroid. Am J Med 2005; 118:706.[PubMed 15989900]

mechanism: Sodium-Glucose Cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) Inhibitors may increase hypoglycemic effects of Insulin.
clinical_effect: Sodium-Glucose Cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) Inhibitors may increase hypoglycemic effects of Insulin.
management: Consider a decrease in insulin dose when initiating therapy with a sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitor and monitor patients for hypoglycemia.
discussion:
In a systematic review evaluating the effects of sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors on insulin dose requirements, 8 out of 12 studies that compared the change in total daily insulin dose (TDI) between the SGLT2 inhibitor group and placebo reported a significant decrease in TDI in the SGLT2 inhibitor group for all study time points.1 Insulin dose reductions ranged from 0.72 units/day to 19.2 units/day.1 In contrast, in a small retrospective study of 27 patients treated with insulin, the initiation of a SGLT2 inhibitor resulted in no significant change in insulin dose, although hypoglycemia did occur in 11% of patients.2
Prescribing information for all SGLT2 inhibitors warn that the incidence of hypoglycemia increases when coadministered with insulin.3,4,5,6,7,8 It is recommended to consider insulin dose reductions in patients receiving both agents and to monitor for the development of hypoglycemia.3,4,5,6,7,8
The mechanism of this interaction is due to additive blood glucose lowering effects.
footnotes:
1. Moulton MK, Johnson BR, Lavender DL, et al. A Scoping Review Evaluating the Effect of SGLT-2 Inhibitors on Insulin Dose Requirements in Insulin-Dependent Patients With Type 2 Diabetes. Ann Pharmacother 2022; 56:1030.[PubMed 35040335]
2. George J, Lobkovich A, Nardolillo J, et al. Real-world evaluation of insulin requirements after GLP1 agonist or SGLT2 inhibitor initiation and titration. Am J Health Syst Pharm 2022; 79:1151.[PubMed 35136945]
3. Invokana (Canagliflozin) [product labeling]. Titusville, NJ, USA: Janssen Pharmaceuticals Inc, October 2022.
4. Farxiga (Dapagliflozin) [product labeling]. Princeton, NJ, USA: Bristol-Myers Squibb Company, October 2022.
5. Jardiance (Empagliflozin) [product labeling]. Ridgefield, CT, USA: Boehringer Ingelheim Pharmaceuticals Inc, October 2022.

8. Inpefa (Sotagliflozin) [product labeling]. The Woodlands, TX, USA: Lexicon Pharmaceuticals, Inc., May 2023.

mechanism: Sucralfate may decrease serum concentrations of Digoxin. Specifically, sucralfate may decrease the absorption of digoxin.
clinical_effect: Sucralfate may decrease serum concentrations of Digoxin. Specifically, sucralfate may decrease the absorption of digoxin.
management: Administer digoxin at least 2 hours before sucralfate. Concomitant administration should be avoided. Monitor for decreased digoxin levels/effects with initiation of sucralfate therapy. Monitor for increased digoxin levels/effects with discontinuation of sucralfate therapy.
route_dependency: This interaction only applies to orally administered digoxin.
discussion:
A single case report describes a 71 year old female with impaired absorption of digoxin, quinidine, and warfarin after administration of sucralfate.1 Subtherapeutic digoxin and quinidine levels as well as decreased prothrombin time (PT) were noted in this patient. Upon sucralfate discontinuation, increases in PT as well as increased serum levels of digoxin, warfarin, and quinidine were observed.
Sucralfate prescribing information states the potential drug-drug interaction with digoxin can be avoided by administering digoxin 2 hours prior to sucralfate.2 The mechanism behind this interaction most likely involves sucralfate binding to digoxin within the gastrointestinal tract and thereby reducing digoxin absorption.
footnotes:
1. Rey AM, Gums JG. Altered absorption of digoxin, sustained-release quinidine, and warfarin with sucralfate administration. DICP 1991; 25:745.[PubMed 1949932]
2. Carafate (Sucralfate) [product labeling]. Madison, NJ, USA: Allergan USA Inc, June 2018.

mechanism: Sucralfate may decrease serum concentrations of Furosemide. Sucralfate may impair the absorption of furosemide.
clinical_effect: Sucralfate may decrease serum concentrations of Furosemide. Sucralfate may impair the absorption of furosemide.
management: Separate orally administered furosemide from sucralfate administration by at least 2 hours.
route_dependency: This interaction only applies to orally administered furosemide.
discussion:
Data evaluating coadministration of furosemide and sucralfate are not available, but the prescribing information for furosemide tablets states coadministration may decrease furosemide efficacy (eg, natriuresis and antihypertensive effects).1
Furosemide prescribing information recommends separating administration of furosemide tablets and sucralfate by at least 2 hours, and monitoring patients receiving this combination for adequate clinical response to furosemide.1
The mechanism of this potential interaction is binding of furosemide to sucralfate in gastrointestinal tract to form a poorly-absorbable complex.
footnotes: 1. Furosemide Tablets [product labeling]. Somerset, NJ, USA: Solco Healthcare US, LLC, April 2020.

mechanism: Ticagrelor may increase serum concentrations of Digoxin.
clinical_effect: Ticagrelor may increase serum concentrations of Digoxin.
management: Monitor for increased digoxin concentrations and effects in patients receiving ticagrelor.
discussion:
In a pharmacokinetic study of 18 healthy volunteers, ticagrelor (400 mg daily for 16 days) increased the digoxin (0.25 mg daily for 7 days) Cmax and AUC 75% and 28%, respectively.1,2
Ticagrelor prescribing information states that digoxin concentrations should be monitored when initiating or discontinuing ticagrelor therapy.2
The presumed primary mechanism of this interaction is due to ticagrelor-mediated inhibition of p-glycoprotein (P-gp), a transporter responsible for digoxin disposition. Of note, the dose of ticagrelor used in this study (400 mg daily) was more than 2 times greater than the typical ticagrelor daily dose (90 mg twice daily). Therefore, the effect of ticagrelor on P-gp and digoxin exposure may be significantly lower in clinical practice when used at typical doses than what was observed in this study.
footnotes:
1. Teng R, Butler K. A pharmacokinetic interaction study of ticagrelor and digoxin in healthy volunteers. Eur J Clin Pharmacol 2013; 69:1801.[PubMed 23748750]
2. Brilinta (Ticagrelor) [product labeling]. Wilmington, DE, USA: AstraZeneca Pharmaceuticals LP, April 2019.

mechanism: Piperacillin may increase nephrotoxic effects of Vancomycin.
clinical_effect: Piperacillin may increase nephrotoxic effects of Vancomycin.
management: Consider monitoring patients closely for evidence of acute kidney injury if using this combination.
discussion:
In a prospective, observational study of 242 patients receiving vancomycin, the incidence of acute kidney injury (AKI) was significantly greater in patients who received piperacillin/tazobactam compared with patients who received cefepime or meropenem (29.8% vs 8.8%; p < 0.001).1 In a retrospective study of 474 pediatric patients, the incidence of AKI was significantly higher among patients who received vancomycin plus piperacillin/tazobactam compared with patients who received vancomycin plus an alternative antipseudomonal beta-lactam (27% vs.7%).2 Use of piperacillin/tazobactam was associated with a significant risk for the development of AKI (OR 6.0, [95% CI, 2.7 to 12.8]).2 In a retrospective chart review of 125 patients (aged 20 to 89 years) receiving vancomycin therapy, nephrotoxicity (defined as an increase in serum creatinine by 0.5 mg/dL or 50% above baseline) occurred in 17 patients.3 Thirteen patients in the nephrotoxicity group received concomitant piperacillin/tazobactam treatment compared to 45 patients in the group without nephrotoxicity [76.5% vs. 41.7%, respectively; adjusted OR=5.4 (95% CI: 1.4 to 20.5)].
In a retrospective cohort study of 191 vancomycin-treated patients with normal baseline renal function, nephrotoxicity (defined as an increase in serum creatinine 50% above baseline) occurred in 8.1% and 16.3% of patients treated with vancomycin alone compared to vancomycin with piperacillin/tazobactam.4 An increased risk of nephrotoxicity was associated with trough concentrations of 15 mg/L or greater (OR=3.67) in both groups. mechanism: Digoxin may decrease anticoagulant effects of Heparin.
clinical_effect: Digoxin may decrease anticoagulant effects of Heparin.
management: No action needed beyond typical PTT or anti-Xa monitoring that accompanies the use of heparin.
discussion: The heparin prescribing information states digoxin may decrease the anticoagulant effects of heparin.1 The mechanism of this potential interaction is unknown.
footnotes: 1. Heparin [product labeling]. Deerfield, IL, USA: Baxter Healthcare Corporation, July 2019.

mechanism: Nitroglycerin may increase antiplatelet effects of Aspirin. Aspirin may increase serum concentrations of Nitroglycerin.
clinical_effect: Nitroglycerin may increase antiplatelet effects of Aspirin. Aspirin may increase serum concentrations of Nitroglycerin.
management: No action required beyond standard clinical care measures.
discussion:
A clinical study in 7 healthy volunteers showed a significant increase in single-dose nitroglycerin (0.8 mg single sublingual dose) concentrations with 1 g aspirin pretreatment (1 hour prior to nitroglycerin) but not with a lower dose regimen (500 mg every other day for 8 days).1 Another report similarly found increases in nitroglycerin Cmax and AUC with higher aspirin doses (1 g) but not lower (500 mg every other day) doses.2 A study of 40 healthy volunteers found no effect of aspirin on nitroglycerin hemodynamic responses (blood pressure and pulse).3
In addition to the potential for aspirin to increase nitroglycerin exposure, a study of 10 healthy volunteers found intravenous nitroglycerin (0.25 mcg/kg/min titrated to a maximum of 3 mcg/kg/min) coadministered with aspirin (500 mg single dose) further reduced platelet aggregation, by approximately the same magnitude as aspirin alone.4 The clinical significance of these findings is unknown.
The mechanism by which aspirin may increase nitroglycerin concentrations is unknown.
footnotes:
1. Weber S, Rey E, Pipeau C, et al. Influence of aspirin on the hemodynamic effects of sublingual nitroglycerin. J Cardiovasc Pharmacol 1983; 5:874.[PubMed 6195479]
2. Rey E, El-Assaf HD, Richard MO, et al. Pharmacological interaction between nitroglycerin and aspirin after acute and chronic aspirin treatment of healthy subjects. Eur J Clin Pharmacol 1983; 25:779.[PubMed 6420164]
3. Levin RI, Feit F. The effect of aspirin on the hemodynamic response to nitroglycerin. Am Heart J 1988; 116:77.[PubMed 3134810]
4. Karlberg KE, Ahlner J, Henriksson P, et al. Effects of nitroglycerin on platelet aggregation beyond the effects of acetylsalicylic acid in healthy subjects. Am J Cardiol 1993; 71:361.[PubMed 8427189]

mechanism: Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate may increase serum concentrations of Furosemide.
clinical_effect: Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate may increase serum concentrations of Furosemide.
management: No action is typically required when sodium zirconium cyclosilicate is coadministered with furosemide. If any increase in furosemide exposure is highly undesirable, consider separating the administration of these agents by at least 2 hours to further minimize the interaction.
route_dependency: This interaction only applies to orally administered furosemide.
discussion:
In a pharmacokinetic study described in the sodium zirconium cyclosilicate prescribing information, coadministration of sodium zirconium cyclosilicate with furosemide (20 mg single dose) increased the furosemide maximum serum concentration approximately 60%, but had no effect on the furosemide AUC.1
The mechanism of this interaction is thought to be due to the temporary elevations in gastric pH that occur with the administration of sodium zirconium cyclosilicate.1 Because of this transient elevation in gastric pH, medications that are weak acids (eg, atorvastatin, furosemide, warfarin) are more readily absorbed when given concomitantly with sodium zirconium cyclosilicate.1
footnotes: 1. Lokelma (Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate) [product labeling]. Wilmington, DE, USA: AstraZeneca Pharmaceuticals LP, May 2018.

mechanism: Sucralfate may decrease serum concentrations of Phenytoin.
clinical_effect: Sucralfate may decrease serum concentrations of Phenytoin.
management: Consider monitoring phenytoin concentrations more closely when starting or stopping sucralfate therapy.
route_dependency: Only oral preparations of phenytoin are expected to participate in this interaction.
discussion:
In clinical studies of healthy volunteers, simultaneous coadministration of sucralfate with phenytoin decreased the phenytoin AUC by approximately 10% to 20%.1,2 In contrast, in another study, steady-state phenytoin serum concentrations were unaffected by sucralfate (1 g four times daily).3
The phenytoin prescribing information notes that sucralfate may decrease phenytoin concentrations and the addition or withdrawal of sucralfate in patients on phenytoin therapy may require an adjustment of the phenytoin dose to achieve optimal clinical outcome.4
The most likely mechanism of this interaction is phenytoin binding to sucralfate in the gastrointestinal tract, limiting its absorption.
footnotes:
1. Smart HL, Somerville KW, Williams J, et al. The effects of sucralfate upon phenytoin absorption in man. Br J Clin Pharmacol 1985; 20:238.[PubMed 3840029]
2. Hall TG, Cuddy PG, Glass CJ, Melethil S. Effect of sucralfate on phenytoin bioavailability. Drug Intell Clin Pharm 1986; 20:607.[PubMed 3755676]
3. Malli R, Jones WN, Rindone JP, Labadie EL. The effect of sucralfate on the steady-state serum concentrations of phenytoin. Drug Metabol Drug Interact 1989; 7:287.[PubMed 2489199]
4. Dilantin (Phenytoin) [product labeling]. New York, NY, USA: Pfizer Inc, March 2022.

mechanism: Digoxin may increase anticoagulant effects of Warfarin.
clinical_effect: Digoxin may increase anticoagulant effects of Warfarin.
management: No action required beyond standard clinical care measures.
discussion:
A single case report describes a 52-year-old woman with known rheumatic heart disease who was admitted to the hospital in acute heart failure secondary to valvular heart disease (severe MS, severe MR, enlarged left atrium compromising right atrium, moderate AR, and moderate TR).1 Her baseline INR was 1.6 and her renal function was normal. She was initiated on torsemide 20 mg, spironolactone 50 mg, digoxin 0.25 mg 5 days/week, warfarin 5 mg, and amoxicillin/clavulanic acid 1,000 mg twice daily. Several days later, she complained of nausea, had an EKG consistent with digoxin toxicity, had a serum digoxin level of 3.6 ng/mL (therapeutic range 0.8 to 2 ng/mL) and her INR was elevated at 3.16. Her digoxin and warfarin were held, and while her digoxin levels normalized, her INR continued to rise to a peak of greater than 10, with no bleeding complications.1
The authors of this report suggest that the elevated digoxin levels lead to her elevated INR and coagulopathy.1 However, the patient’s elevated baseline INR, acute heart failure with likely hepatic congestion, and concomitant use of amoxicillin/clavulanic acid are all factors more likely to contribute to an elevated INR than digoxin. The role of digoxin in the development of over anticoagulation in this patient is very unclear.
footnotes: 1. Raina R, Kaushik M, Mahajan S, et al. Rare Interaction of Warfarin and Digoxin in a Case of Digoxin Toxicity. J Assoc Physicians India 2020; 68:85.[PubMed 32138495]

mechanism: Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate may increase serum concentrations of Warfarin.
clinical_effect: Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate may increase serum concentrations of Warfarin.
management: Separate the administration of sodium zirconium cyclosilicate and warfarin by at least 2 hours. If simultaneous administration is required, monitor for signs and symptoms of warfarin toxicity (eg, elevated INR, bleeding).
discussion:
In a pharmacokinetic study of healthy volunteers, coadministration of sodium zirconium cyclosilicate with racemic warfarin (5 mg single dose) increased the S-warfarin AUC and maximum serum concentration (Cmax) approximately 15% and 40%, respectively, and increased the R-warfarin AUC and Cmax approximately 10% and 30%, respectively.1
The mechanism of this interaction is thought to be temporary elevations in gastric pH that occur with the administration of sodium zirconium cyclosilicate.2 Because of this transient elevation in gastric pH, medications that are weak acids (eg, atorvastatin, furosemide, warfarin) are more readily absorbed when given concomitantly with sodium zirconium cyclosilicate.2
footnotes:
1. Någård M, Kramer WG, Boulton DW. Evaluation of potential drug interactions with sodium zirconium cyclosilicate: a single-center, open-label, one sequence crossover study in healthy adults. Clin Kidney J 2021; 14:1808.[PubMed 34221388]
2. Lokelma (Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate) [product labeling]. Wilmington, DE, USA: AstraZeneca Pharmaceuticals LP, May 2018.
